What it measures: Core coverage: the video teaches the lesson's concepts using the lesson's own examples, well enough to substitute for reading. Weighted heaviest because the narration spine is the one thing edits can't manufacture.
What we can do: Re-roll. A weak spine can't be repaired; a strong spine makes everything else patchable.

What it measures: Whether each kit board's points get taught, not whether the jpg appears pixel-exact. A board that never shows costs nothing if its content is fully covered (canon: why-learn-ai's everyday-apps board redrawn as 5 scenes = no deduction). Exact display is still the reliable way to score full marks; prompts still demand it (7/13 amendment changed grading, not prompting).
What we can do: Insert the board ourselves to match the narration (re-walk from kit jpgs).

What it measures: Animation is preferred over static images. It makes it a more compelling experience; long still holds read as dead spots.
What we can do: Re-roll, or graft donor motion scenes over the stills. Ken Burns only fakes it.

What it measures: Gibberish/pseudo-text costs points only on content-carrying visuals (garbled label on a teaching chart); incidental b-roll props are forgiven. Also legibility, text containment, and white-on-light (hard visual fail, graded BAD even in transitions).
What we can do: Patch visuals: clone-freeze, graft, or masked restore (drawbox over invented figures).

What it measures: Runtime and flow; no padding, dead spots, or final-scene starvation.
What we can do: Trim; replace still holds with donor motion.

What it measures: Missing a hard lesson requirement in the narration disqualifies a roll as spine (e.g. document-trap must say "Retrieval-Augmented Generation" in full; required verbatim lines).
What we can do: Nearly unfixable — the one true roll-killer. A cross-roll audio graft can occasionally rescue it when another roll speaks the line (support-trap 7/24).

What it measures: Profanity legible on screen, depiction of a real person against a by-name ban, or banned imagery classes (self-harm, medical, red-staining where fenced). Costs nothing against the roll's score but must NEVER reach students — this is a course for 16-year-olds.
What we can do: Patch or replace every violating span before ship; read every legible text span at full resolution during mapping.

What it measures: Getty/watermarked stock can never ship regardless of score (unlicensed; cropping the watermark is worse, not better).
What we can do: Insert illustrations, graft an alternative scene, or delete the span.

What it measures: Ships only ending on the close board as the literal final frame: no outro, no engine-drawn sign restating the message (named banned class).
What we can do: Insert our own close board over the final narration (freeze finisher / composed close).

What it measures: A/V sync: elements appear as narration mentions them; narration leads, visuals follow.
What we can do: Edit to sync: visual-replace over the untouched narration.

What it measures: Dotted paper, paper-craft, live-action hands, off-palette colors are tolerated; mention at most as one-line color in intake reports.
David can waive specific violations after watching; note waivers in the report and the sheet's Reason cell.

r2, adopted 2026-07-24. Score /100 = roll-as-raw-material quality (spine value); ship gates are binary checks on the FINAL artifact after repairs. Grades under the old boards-30 rubric (catalog 72-96) are NOT comparable — tag new grades r2 in the sheet's Reason cell; re-grade a video only when it's touched.
